title: share: Assuming $pmux inputs are onehot is unsound.
body:
## Steps to reproduce the issue

Suppose we have a contrived ALU where the opcode interpretation is determined by a mode input, and in one case it is one-hot:

```
module my_alu(
        input [2:0] op,
        input mode,
        input [3:0] A,
        input [3:0] B,
        input [3:0] C,
        output [3:0] X,
        output [3:0] Y,
);

always @* begin
        if (mode) begin
                (* full_case, parallel_case *)
                casez (op)
                        3'b??1: begin
                                X = A & B;
                                Y = A & C;
                        end
                        3'b?1?: begin
                                X = A | B;
                                Y = A | C;
                        end
                        3'b1??: begin
                                X = A ^ B;
                                Y = A ^ C;
                        end
                endcase
        end else begin
                if (op[0])
                        X = A * B;
                else
                        X = 0;
                if (op[1])
                        Y = A * C;
                else
                        Y = 0;
        end
end

endmodule
```

Running `proc; opt_clean; eval -set mode 0 -set op 3 -set A 1 -set B 2 -set C 3` on it, we supply valid input values, and obtain correct output:

```
Eval result: \X = 4'0010.
Eval result: \Y = 4'0011.
```

## Expected behavior

Running `share` before eval should not change the (well-defined) behavior of this circuit.

## Actual behavior

Running `proc; opt_clean; share; eval -set mode 0 -set op 3 -set A 1 -set B 2 -set C 3` on the above example, we get mis-optimized circuit with incorrect output:

```
Eval result: \X = 4'0011.
Eval result: \Y = 4'0011.
```

This is because there is a `$pmux` cell in the netlist that has `op` as an input, and `share` incorrectly infers from that that `op` must always be one-hot, and thus the `if (op[0])` and `if (op[1])` branches can never be true at the same time, then decides to merge the two multiplier cells into one multiplexed cell.
